content.text: , pronounced 'salsa') is a security framework that defines what claims a build pipeline must be able to make — and prove — about how an artifact was produced. A SLSA provenance attestation answers: which source commit produced this artifact, on which build service, with what inputs, and has the artifact been modified since?

SLSA provenance doesn't prevent breaches in your code — it prevents undetected tampering with your build pipeline. If an attacker injects malicious code between your source commit and your production image, SLSA provenance makes that injection detectable.

This sub-subtopic covers SLSA levels 1–3, how to generate provenance attestations with the GitHub SLSA provenance generator, how to verify attestations at deploy time, and what build hardening is required to reach SLSA level 3.

attrs.alt: SLSA levels 1-4 and the build provenance chain: source SHA → build run ID → artifact digest → attestation envelope → verification

content.text: SLSA defines four levels of increasing supply-chain security. Each level adds requirements on top of the previous:
Build service is hardened; no network access during build

SLSA Level 3 is the practical target for most Claude applications: it provides meaningful supply-chain integrity without requiring hermetic build infrastructure that is difficult to operate. L3 requires that the build service be
— provenance is generated by GitHub Actions itself, not by the workflow author's code.
Generating SLSA L3 Provenance with GitHub Actions
The SLSA GitHub generator is a reusable workflow maintained by the SLSA team. It runs as a separate job that receives the artifact digest and signs provenance using Sigstore's keyless OIDC signing:

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release with SLSA Provenance

on:
  push:
    tags: ['v*']

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build and push Docker image
        id: build
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          push: true
          tags: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}:${{ github.ref_name }}
          # Output the digest for provenance
        # digest output: sha256:abc123...

  provenance:
    needs: [build]
    permissions:
      actions: read          # read the workflow run
      id-token: write        # OIDC token for Sigstore signing
      packages: write        # attach attestation to OCI registry
    uses: slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0
    with:
      image: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}
      digest: ${{ needs.build.outputs.image-digest }}
      registry-username: ${{ github.actor }}
    secrets:
      registry-password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

The `provenance` job runs in a separate, isolated environment controlled by the SLSA GitHub generator — not by your workflow. This is what makes it L3: the build service (GitHub Actions) generates the provenance, not the workflow author.
The provenance attestation is a DSSE (Dead Simple Signing Envelope) containing a SLSA provenance predicate. It records every input that could have affected the build:
field pins the exact source tree. The
field identifies the SLSA generator version. Any discrepancy between these fields and what the Rekor transparency log records indicates tampering.
Verifying provenance before deploying to production closes the loop — the build process proves what it produced; the deploy process proves that what it's deploying matches that proof:

# Install slsa-verifier (pinned version)
curl -sSfL https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-verifier/releases/download/v2.6.0/slsa-verifier-linux-amd64 \
  -o /usr/local/bin/slsa-verifier && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/slsa-verifier

# Verify provenance before deploy
IMAGE="ghcr.io/org/claude-api"
DIGEST="sha256:abc123def456..."

slsa-verifier verify-image "${IMAGE}@${DIGEST}" \
  --source-uri "github.com/org/claude-api" \
  --source-tag "v1.42.0" \
  --builder-id "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0"

# Output on success:
# Verified build using builder "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/..."
# at source "github.com/org/claude-api" tag "v1.42.0"
# PASSED: Verified SLSA provenance

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml — verify before deploying
name: Deploy to Production

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      image_digest:
        description: 'Image digest to deploy (sha256:...)'
        required: true

jobs:
  verify-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    steps:
      - name: Install slsa-verifier
        run: |
          curl -sSfL https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-verifier/releases/download/v2.6.0/slsa-verifier-linux-amd64 \
            -o /usr/local/bin/slsa-verifier && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/slsa-verifier

      - name: Verify SLSA provenance
        run: |
          slsa-verifier verify-image \
            "ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}@${{ inputs.image_digest }}" \
            --source-uri "github.com/${{ github.repository }}" \
            --builder-id "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0"
          echo "Provenance verified — proceeding with deploy"

      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/claude-api \
            app=ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}@${{ inputs.image_digest }}
Build Hardening Requirements for SLSA L3
SLSA L3 requires that the build environment be hardened against tampering. The SLSA GitHub generator enforces most of this automatically, but your build workflow must also follow these practices:
Pin all action versions to SHA
Mutable tag pointing to malicious code
No write access to source repo during build
provenance job in separate permissions context
Workflow author forging their own provenance

# Hardened build job — minimum required permissions
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read      # read source only
      packages: write     # push image
      # No id-token here — that's in the provenance job only
    steps:
      # Pin ALL actions to full SHA (not just tag)
      - uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683  # v4.2.2
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@c47758b77c9736f4b2ef4073d4d51994fabfe349  # v3.7.1
      - uses: docker/build-push-action@4f58ea79222b3b9dc2c8bbdd6debcef730109a75  # v6.9.0
        with:
          push: true
          # No --network=host; no external downloads during build
          build-args: |
            BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE=1
Enforcing SLSA Verification as a Kubernetes Admission Policy
Generating and verifying provenance in CI is a good start, but it's opt-in — a deployment that skips verification still works.
enforce verification at the Kubernetes layer, blocking any image that lacks a valid SLSA attestation before it can be scheduled:

# kyverno-slsa-policy.yml — Kyverno policy enforcing SLSA attestation
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-slsa-provenance
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-slsa-attestation
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds: [Pod]
              namespaces: [prod]
      verifyImages:
        - imageReferences:
            - "ghcr.io/org/claude-api*"
          attestations:
            - predicateType: "https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1"
              attestors:
                - entries:
                    - keyless:
                        subject: "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@refs/tags/v2.0.0"
                        issuer: "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
              conditions:
                - all:
                    - key: "{{ predicate.buildDefinition.resolvedDependencies[0].uri }}"
                      operator: Equals
                      value: "git+https://github.com/org/claude-api@refs/heads/main"

With this policy applied, Kubernetes rejects any pod in the `prod` namespace that references an image without a valid SLSA L3 attestation from the trusted builder. The keyless verification uses Sigstore's public Fulcio CA — no certificate management required.
A compliance script audits recent image tags and reports which ones have valid provenance attestations — useful for board-level supply chain reporting:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""audit_slsa_compliance.py — report SLSA attestation status for recent images."""
import subprocess, json, os, sys

IMAGE_BASE = os.environ.get("IMAGE_BASE", "ghcr.io/org/claude-api")
TAGS_FILE  = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "deployed-tags.txt"
BUILDER_ID = "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0"

def verify(image: str, tag: str) -> bool:
    result = subprocess.run(
        [
            "slsa-verifier", "verify-image",
            f"{image}:{tag}",
            "--source-uri", "github.com/org/claude-api",
            "--builder-id", BUILDER_ID,
        ],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    return result.returncode == 0

def main() -> None:
    with open(TAGS_FILE) as f:
        tags = [line.strip() for line in f if line.strip()]

    results = []
    for tag in tags:
        ok = verify(IMAGE_BASE, tag)
        results.append({"tag": tag, "slsa_l3": ok})
        status = "PASS" if ok else "FAIL"
        print(f"[{status}] {IMAGE_BASE}:{tag}")

    passed = sum(1 for r in results if r["slsa_l3"])
    total  = len(results)
    print(f"\nCompliance: {passed}/{total} images have valid SLSA L3 provenance")
    if passed < total:
        print("FAIL: Some images lack valid SLSA provenance — investigate before next audit")
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Run this script quarterly as part of your supply-chain security review. Store the output alongside the SBOM archive so that both artefact inventory and build provenance records are available for compliance reporting.
is the practical target: provenance generated by the build service (not the workflow author), signed via Sigstore's keyless OIDC, and verifiable by anyone with the artifact digest
runs as a separate reusable workflow job — isolated from the build job — and signs provenance using the GitHub Actions OIDC token
records source commit SHA, workflow path, builder ID, and run metadata in a DSSE envelope attached to the OCI image
at deploy time confirms the image digest matches the signed provenance and that the builder ID is the trusted SLSA generator
: pin all action SHAs, use contents:read only, keep secrets out of build jobs, use the provenance job's separate permissions context
makes provenance publicly auditable — any omission or modification of the attestation is detectable by comparing against the immutable log
